speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you happen to come to Moscow in the first place? Do you understand?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, our trip, our reason for coming to Moscow was a neighbor of ours that had moved a couple of years before here and he wrote my father. There was an opening as a meat cutter with a hagen, cushion and holt. So we immediately got packed up, took the door to the Pacific and arrived in Moscow in March, 1902. A wet rainy day, it was muddy and miserable but we were able to stay overnight with the people that told us about the position and then the Jackson across the street from the old pleasant home boarding house rented the house for twelve hours a month until we could build a house of our own. Moscow was kind of divided into a special section for the Scandinavian people and it was called Swedetown and of course his father being Scandinavian immediately looked for a place up there. Well, you couldn't buy a lot, you had to buy an acre. So he bought an acre and built a house on it, a three bedroom house for twelve hundred dollars and the ground was two hundred dollars. The neighbor next door was a contractor, he built the house, Gustaf Johnson and his son Theodore. Then in order to get the place landscaped and fixed up he got Alan Ramstead, whose father, Victor Ramstead, to plant thirty -two fruit trees of his own choice on the place. And then a barn was built where we kept a cow, chickens and a pig and remember this was only five blocks from Main Street so it wasn't very far from town. You could do those things in those days. We had to have an outhouse because there were no sewer facilities and we had to dig our own well for our own water. And that lasted until about 1910 or 1912 before we got water and sewer connected to the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would that be today? About what street?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Quarter of Logan and 7th. Then in 1905, I think it was, we moved out to the packing plant, Hagan and Christian packing plant where our father could transfer from a meat cutter to a butcher. And we lived there and you could make more money because you got a free house, free milk, free meat, free fuel, free everything. All he had to buy was fuel and necessities to wear and so on. Well from there he, we were there for about two years and when he moved to town, he passed away in 1907.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So you were growing up right during that time, you were just a boy. Well I was nine years old when I came to Moscow so I remember a good deal of the early history of Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about how you played as a boy and the kinds of things that the kids did in town then?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well I think the old kids games of run sheep run, hide and go seek, baseball, kick the can, all those games were very popular. Baseball was a very popular sport with us. We played it in cow pastures and every place around. So it was a real game. But the social life, when I was around from oh I'd say sixteen on, was pretty much connected with the churches, the young peoples. Being a Lutheran we had our young people's society there. A little later on we had a roller rink which we used. Fourth of July's were a big event in Moscow. Practically all of it took place on Main Street and the races, horse races and so on at the fairgrounds. There was one particular race that they had on Main Street on the Fourth of July, I always remember, they put up standards with a ring. I would judge about two inches in diameter hung from these standards and then the man on horseback had to make it in a certain time down the street with a billiard cue and grab those rings and the one that got the most rings got the prize. They also had to make it in a certain time if they didn't have a certain time while they were eliminated. And of course Main Street was decorated with all kinds of muttings and there were hot dog stands and various concessions on Main Street. A lot of youngsters races.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And believe it or not, before I think along about five to ten we had a minor league baseball team here and they competed with Pullman, Colfax, Palouse, Lewiston, the town's round here and all the players with exception of the pitchers and the catcher were unpaid. And some of the people that played on the team, those volunteers were Gov. Nix who played shortstop, Roy Holman who was connected with the Washington Water Power and Hal Tilly who was the university student. This all took place down on the fairgrounds where the Rosar place is now which later become the International Higher Hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the early fire at the university? You were a kid when that happened, right?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Let's see, that happened in 1907 I think or six, six I guess it was. It was right in there someplace. We were living on a farm at the time and we were notified that the university was on fire so we went up the top of the hill and saw it from about three miles away. After the building burned down they wanted to salvage the brick and they employed a lot of us youngsters to clean brick. They dynamited the building and oh, there must have been twenty of us up there cleaning brick and we were paid by a stack of one hundred. We stacked them up on one hundred. Well, during the cleaning of the brick there was a strike which I and my partner didn't strike and the foreman put us on an easy brick pile where all we had to do was to scrape the brick together and pile them up. In that way we made sixty -five dollars apiece just cleaning brick at the university. And it was long after that while the legislature provided money to start a new university.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Later on, connected by the university, after my father passed away I worked for George Creighton as a messenger and stock keeper. And in about a year he figured that I ought to learn something about merchandising so he got somebody else to deliver packages and I worked in the store as stock work and eventually started selling merchandise. They were very good to me, both Mr. Creighton and his wife. And it was one of my chores every Monday morning to go up to Mrs. Creighton's house to get her laundry and take it down to a lady, Mrs. Rosaboe, who did her laundry for her.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then along about 1910 there were a couple of young men down at David's store that quit, had a disagreement with the firm and they both quit. One of the fellows happened to be staying with my mother as a rumor and a lawyer and he told me about it and he said if you want the job go down and see Mr. David. So I immediately did that and I went down there and I, being Scandinavian, why, and the county at that time was around sixty to seventy percent Scandinavian anyway and a lot of them didn't talk very good English. So he employed me at once as a salesman in the men's department. Well, that went against the grain of Mr. Creighton and he thought he was going to keep me there for years I guess and he didn't speak to me for years afterwards and even called up Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: David and told him, he says, what do you mean by taking my help away from him?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So that ended that. Well, I was in the store in the men's department, the shoe department, the boys department, up until World War I. Mr. Homer David was head of the department and so he wanted to take over the raid. We were in the driver's department and he put me in his head at the men's department in 1917 I think it was. And I held that position until the time I retired in 1958.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let me ask you this, when you first started working there for Creighton's did you have a pretty good idea in your mind that this was the kind of work you were going to want to do?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh yes, I just loved merchandising, I loved merchandising and I liked meeting people. It was easy for me to wait on people for that reason and all the time that I connected with the store, unless there were people that didn't have a charge account, I practically knew them by their first name because the population at that time of Moscow was round about 3 ,500 and there were only about 600 enrolled at the university. So we got to know every faculty member and practically every student.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: One thing that the Davids prided themselves on and all the history of their experience in the store, they did a tremendous credit business because they had a grocery department, a furniture department, it was the biggest department store in northern Idaho and they extended credit rather freely to people. And all the time they did credit,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: the students, any number of students would get credit in the fall of the year and they'd operate and clear until the next fall, wouldn't pay their bill until the next fall. And they paid it up in the fall by working during the summer. And the farmers did the same thing, a lot of the farmers paid up once a year. Well the results of that were just one tenth of one percent of their credit with students. Now let me create it, one percent, not one tenth. And the farmers were greater than that. Farmers were at greater risk than students at that time, which was quite a coincidence. Speaking about the store being the biggest store in northern Idaho, they had a tremendous exchange of, bought eggs, bacon and butter from the farmers, they brought it in. And for that they got script money, values from five cents to five dollars. And some of these people would collect a hundred or more dollars worth of that script to pay up some of the bills that they had. I've seen many a times when they were candling eggs, they would get in on a Friday and Saturday, dozens and dozens of eggs, and they'd have to candle them so that there weren't any spoiled eggs. And I've seen lots of eggs that were spoiled eggs and rotten eggs that the people had picked up eggs nests along fence rows or something and turned them in. And butter, some of it you could smell all of the time it got in the store and they took that and they were baking. And the surplus of eggs, we got in more eggs than we could even sell locally. We put them in thirty dozen cases and shipped them up to Spokane for the refrigerator for the refrigerator up there. And in the season of the year that we needed those, then we drew on them and bought them back. So that was quite a business with bacon and eggs and butter. And the farmers in those days, in the time that I was in the store even, in 1910s when I went in there, the automobile still wasn't in use and the farmers only came to town where they were off five or six or ten miles away from town. They'd only come in two or three times a year. And most of these farmers had big families, five, six, eight, and ten children, and bring them in a hay rack or a hack of some kind, come in the store, and we'd fit every one of the younger gents, underwear, sweaters, shoes, socks, clothing, for the winter. And of course, they all attended country schools, so our city schools are just for people in Moscow. So it was quite a chore. It took a good part of a half a day to wait on one family to fit them out.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: This would be when the farmer would only come in a couple few times a year. So his trading trip was a real major experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would most of these farmers tend to trade locally more than they would in Moscow, let's say? Would they save Moscow for the big trip?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Moscow was a trading center.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There was no other place really for them to trade because in their own communities like Genesee and Uniontown, Colton, and Kendrick and Juliet and those places, they couldn't afford to have the stocks that the Moscow stores could have. So Moscow has always been a trading center of, oh, I would say 30 miles around. Because today Moscow goes clear down to Andikop, up to Tico, Oakesdale, and all those places, Uniontown, Colton. It's still a trading center of this area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first went to work for Davis, what were the styles that were popular that you sold?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I remember when I left Creighton's in Manabu, Davis, Creighton's had hard chaffers and marks closed and they had quite a reputation even at that time. So I bought a blue surge suit. It's very conservative, three button, had a vest, and you wore suspenders instead of a belt. It had a shirt with a white collar, sometimes a hook on tie.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And then along about

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: 1912, young men's styles began to get pretty wild. That is, L -System Clothes of Chicago came out with a suit that was peg tops and about a 14 inch bottom. You could stick your hands in your pant pockets and pull them out like a lady's skirt almost. And the front of the coat dipped. And the front of the dip almost hit your knee in the front. Well, that lasted for about four years. And then we got into the real English model suit, which is the other extreme. Very tight, short coat, three and four button front, and a very tight fitting suit. Well, I was going to the fair in San Francisco in 1915, the World's Fair, and I bought one of those suits and I put it on and it was so extreme that I was afraid to even wear it around Moscow at the time. So when we went to the fair, I put this suit on and I went down the side street, down to the depot. There were three of us that went to the fair, Harvey Fullerton and Walter Johnson.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And when we got down to San Francisco, of course, we felt at home because we saw all those types of suits down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said when you went down the side street, you mean you sort of snuck down to the depot?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah, we went down Washington Street.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So we had quite a lot of experience out of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, by the way, how did the fair strike you down there? Did you enjoy that World's Fair in 1915?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: In fact, we stayed within four or five blocks of the fairgrounds so we spent the whole week out there. It was a big fair. In fact, we enjoyed it very much. We'd go into town in the evening sometimes and one of the things I remember in San Francisco is they had free lunch at the beer halls or beer taverns, I guess you'd call them. And they served steamed beer, a great big pot of it, for five cents. And then you could go over to a buffet and help yourself to cheese and crackers and dried fish and whatnot, all for the five cent drink of beer.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then we went from there down to San Diego and saw the State Fair and on down into Tijuana and Mexico to say we'd been out of the country, enjoyed that. All this trip from here was by train to Portland and from Portland to San Francisco by a new boat, the Northern Pacific, that had just been put into service and came back by boat.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Let me ask you about how you got your stock for the store, David's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did it come from?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, after I'd been there about, oh, five, six years as a senior Mr. David took a liking to me and figured that I was going to be one of them. So he offered me some stock in the company to be paid for out of the dividends that the stock made. Well, that stock had doubled two different times in the time that I had it, so it was more valuable as the years went on. I carried that stock up until 1957 when the stock was sold to Mr. Childers of Spokane.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I still have the stock because Childers didn't buy the stock three years ago and he's paying for that over a period of years and I still get dividends on the stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the clothing? Where did you come by the selection? How did you decide what clothes you were going to carry in the store?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, all through the years a salesman from the factory would come out with big trunks full of samples and suit some of the models already made up and he'd open up in the sample room over at the hotel and we'd spend half a day over there picking out merchandise. I did that twice a year, fall and spring. Then in later years, oh I'd say in the 50s, sometimes they made a third trip to fill in stocks and so on. Most of the clothing was bought from salesmen that called us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that the selection that was available to you was wide?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, we had the same selection as the big city stores. The only difficulty sometimes if you were not called on soon enough, you lost out on some of the patterns that they made up into suits.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: During World War I, we had a lot of the US Army stationed here at the University. They filled up all the Army houses and the dormitories.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: In World War I, one was really in its height. We also had a flu epidemic here, which took hundreds or thousands of people all over the United States. I think Moscow lost something like 90 or 100 people on the flu. Well, the soldiers couldn't come down and shop, so I put on a mask and went up and called on them. I got privilege from the Commandant to do that. During that period, I sold 675 uniforms to soldiers. I've forgotten how many, but it seems like we had about 1 ,800 or 2 ,000 soldiers there. At that time, the soldiers were wearing spiral leggings and high -cut colored uniforms and so on. The uniforms they got were very shoddy material and they weren't satisfied with it. So, the order went out that even a corporal could buy the same kind of material in a uniform as a colonel. So, I went up there then and took measurements. I got a deposit down and when they came in, fitted them, it was quite a boost to our business during those war years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you order the suits from?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, some of them from Redback, New Jersey, and some of the better ones were from Kuppenheimer at Chicago. They went into the uniform business.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: They were one of the better clothing concerns in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see much sickness during that flu epidemic yourself? Did you witness?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You just see one fellow one day and he was gone the next. Some of your best friends. So, the flu was quite severe.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: During the latter part of the epidemic, they found that alcohol, a good shot of bourbon, was about as good an antidote as anything. Of course, we had prohibition on those dyes and it was hard to get. You can get it at a drug store, they had it.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: One thing I was curious about was about how you learned yourself, how to fit clothes and how to sell the clothes to people who came in and how to build up your clientele.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, most of these salesmen that called on us, clothing lines, I got a lot of information from them. Because they were old timers. They were men up in their 50s and 60s that called on us. So they were well versed in the clothing industry and I got a lot of information from them. Then we had a tailor, Frank Yangle, who made suits and also did our altering for us. I observed his knowledge of tailoring, got to know how the inside of a suit was made. I could see him making them. As far as taking measures and so on is concerned, it wasn't long until if you made a mistake some place, you corrected it the next time. So it wasn't too difficult.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: As far as selling is concerned, I found out the best way to handle people on selling was to let them express themselves. Not to be high powered in selling. I think that was a mistake with a lot of salesmen. Customers didn't have very much confidence in them. Then after you have satisfied a customer and he's gotten what you told him it was, he came back. And he wanted you to wait on him again. I found that very true after I retired. That they called me in twice a year for about 12 years on their sales. And of course, I met dozens and dozens of people that I had fitted suits in years before would see me and say, I want to see it. So I think it was building up confidence in yourself and the customer.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And David's were a store that made good on faulty merchandise. If they got something that didn't hold up the way they should, they made it good. And that was a success. And when they turned the store over to Mr. Childers, they turned over a lot of good will that helped him establish himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say that selling the woman was more important than selling the man on the man's clothes usually?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I think women have had influence on men wearing apparel for years and years. I think they have a little better idea of matching a tie to a shirt or a tie to a suit.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And I think you have to appeal to the women in selling a man. Once in a while you get a man that says, no, I'm going to have what I want. But if you appeal to the woman, your job is half done on selling merchandise. Women are pretty smart shoppers and some of them are pretty tough customers to wait on. So you can't contradict them or you're through. You've got to let them know that they know their business. And I think that's success in selling women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any customers that just were real hard boiled and knew just what they wanted and wouldn't take anybody's word for it except theirs?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I'll cite this one woman because she's dead and gone and there's nothing against her. She was born in Sweden and new wool materials, new linen from early childhood. And she had a big family of eight. They were farmers and she'd come in the store to... Her husband even couldn't buy a suit without her okay. And she would examine the material. If I told her it was all wool, she'd say, well, let's see if it's all wool. And she would get to the bottom of the trousers first. She'd get a hold of a piece of yarn and she would pull it out to see whether it was wool or not and how long the wool it was. And then she would even burn it to smell the wool. And when those tests were made, she was satisfied. Well, she did the buying for their youngsters until they were young men and women and left home. But she was a very shrewd buyer. I remember one winter there was a Minnesota Woolenbills come out here with winter underwear. Everybody wore wool underwear in those days practically. And he sold her because it was good all along wool, wool underwear for the family. I don't know when we got away from two -piece underwear. I've kind of forgotten when we got into union suits. And now we're back to two -piece again. Have been for a good many years since the jockey shark came out.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Just one more question about women shoppers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were men usually accompanied by their wives when they came into the store to buy clothing?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The final purchase was, but there's plenty of times that the wife came in before to find out what there was that she thought her husband ought to have. So you usually were selling the clothes to a couple more than to just a... As far as selling a suit, yes. That was in the earlier days. Today it's different because you find a lot of young men that buy their own suits. They're not married a lot of course. Some of them are professional people that just go in and buy a suit and that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did suits cost when you first started working?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well I remember you could get a good all -wool, 60, 14 ounce blue serge suit for around $17 to $20.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now that wasn't the best of tailoring, but you could get the best of tailoring in what was considered the best make suits in those days, around $25 to $35. That was about tops. I have a book down the basement that I kept track of all the suits that we sold from 1910 until 1950 I guess. It's a great one to look over because I can go back and see some of the old, old time customers. And as I have gone through that, I should have had that book up here, but the average sale of suits, and we would sell around 500, 600 suits a year. The average would run around one year, it seems to me like it was around $18 and something, and then it went up to $20 some odd. But we sold a lot of suits for $12, $15.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people have as many occasions to dress formally in those days, in the early days, as they do now? Would they have many suits?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, we did.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I remember even the fraternity houses. This will go back before World War I. You should buy full -dress suits, long -tail suits, wing collars, and all that. And then we had a few social groups in town here, just to have monthly parties, and they would dress up in full -dress suits. And that carried on up until, I would say, about the Depression of 1931 or 1932, and then they switched right over to a tuxedo. And as far as full -dress is concerned, except for professional performers, for dances and social affairs, it's been tuxedos ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sell jackets in those days, or was it all suits?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: All suits. There were no sport coats.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I don't think the sport coat got into the field until after World War II, it seems to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, how about casual clothes, and work clothes? What kind of clothes did you sell for those purposes?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, you didn't have the khaki slacks like you see so much of today, and it was mostly overalls. Striped overalls, blue overalls, and of course of the makes, Levi's Strauss. Levi's were the predominant overall, waist overall. And Camp Buston was another make that was good. Sweet R,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Lees, one made back in Milwaukee, I forgot the name of that one. It was a very popular one. And work shirts, I'm amazed at the price of chambray work shirts that I see on the counter today. I sold hundreds and hundreds of dozens of chambray shirts at 39 cents apiece. And today, a chambray shirt runs around $6. So there's quite a difference there. And of course men's socks, you had the old Rockford sock for work sock. And you wore garters, suspenders, except on Levi's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the Rockford sock made out of?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Cotton, and of course, wool socks in the wintertime. Now these Rockford socks only sold for 5 to 15 cents apiece in those days. Canvas clothes were 5 to 25 cents.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Got a good pair of shoes for 5 hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sell a lot of work boots in those days?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, we had a logging business all around this area. We sold a lot of cocked boots because they needed those in the timber. Because there were a lot of what you call gyppoing in the lumber mills. All those logs had to be drug down to the ponds and the mills and so on. Yeah, we sold a lot of boots. And in those early days, women's and men's shoes were all 6 inch tops. There were no oxfords in those days. And even women's shoes. There was a period there, I think before World War I, when ladies wore laced dress shoes, you'd call them boots, that they had to come clear up below their knees and lace them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about casual clothing? Was there anything in between the work clothes and the dress clothes for just an everyday like we are today? Everyday wear?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we wore sweaters instead of a coat some of the time.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, they did have some odd trousers made out of wool. There were no cotton trousers, but I remember them.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And those sports shirts or anything like that. In fact, I don't remember a shirt with a collar on until up in the 20s. Before that time, you wore a white collar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did the student styles differ from what you sold to the townspeople?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, like today, they were more extreme. Like the L -System suit I was telling you about. Not many of the townspeople bought that type of a suit, but the students did. And as years have gone by, the students have been important in styles of men's clothing. Today, the younger people have set more styles than the older people do. That's where sports wear came in, and they were the ones that originated that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the university was as important to business in the early days as it has become in recent years here?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, when you figure in the difference in population, we only have a population in those early days of, oh, probably not over 4 ,000. Less than that, probably. And if you had a student body of around 600 to 900, that would compare very well with our population today of 15 ,000 and 7 ,000 students. So, I'd say the student business has always been a factor in merchandising here. Farming, of course, was the biggest business we had. And today,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: the university's business is a big factor, and it is in Moscow because we have two universities so close together. There's a tremendous amount of Pullman people trained in Moscow. Not only students, but faculty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your competition in the early days? Did you have much in clothing from other stores?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, we did. We had a merchant come into Moscow named N. Williamson, who opened up a store down where J .C. Penney is now. Much smaller quarters than J .C. Penney. And then later, moved up to where the Tuna Parklets are today. And he had the whole three floors of merchandise. And he was then the biggest merchandiser in Northern Idaho. Overtook Davis. And even had a restroom connected with it so people could lunch there. He had an elevator and all that. And he was a very... he was a promoter. He had sales. And he was an Irishman. He had a lot on the ball. And he was competitive with us. But it may have hurt us some, but it also helped us in the long run because having a store of that type brought more people in from the outside. We had more trading area people.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: He was in business there up until... oh golly. I'd have to look up. It seems to me he was in business there up until just before World War II. But he and another fellow got interested in the stock business. Full-breeded stock business. And he got putting all his money into that. And it affected the store and he finally went all business. His son took part of the stock and opened up over at Palouse. Frank Williamson. He was a Williamson and a promoter. In fact, he was the instigator of one of the first fairs in Moscow. And that's back before 1910. He was quite a man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say in general there was a lot of competition in Moscow among the different businesses?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Creighton's were competitive. They had their clientele.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: They had a certain brand of merchandise that people liked and they went there for it. We had another store, Oscar Silver, that opened up but he didn't last very long. We had the fashion shop, which is Fanny Stewart, early days. He sold out and it was fair competition.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: We had Carter and Haynes, a men's shop, a specialty shop that opened up. But they didn't last too long. The store weathered a lot of those people. Then there was another men's shop opened up. Gibson Road from Lewiston opened up. And they didn't last very long. I went down and bought their stock when they closed out, 25 cents on the dollar and sold it out on sale. So while we had lots of competition over the years, we withstood it all and a lot of them didn't weather it. And the only reason why the store decided to sell out is that they were getting too old. And they felt that while the store was still had a good name, goodwill, that they'd dispose of it. Well, there were two or three chain stores that wanted to buy it. But for the reputation the store had, they didn't want to sell it to a chain. And that's how Mr. Childers happened to get it out of Spokane. He's an independent. And he's operated it now for, well, 16, 17 years. And he's done very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the stock that was carried at David's, how was that different from the stock that was carried at Crayton's and the men's line? What was the difference between the two?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Not much of a difference, except David's had a much bigger stock. We had a bigger clothing stock, we had a bigger shirt stock. I don't think Crayton's had more clothing at all, but they had a shoe department. And they had a rug department. So that a customer wanted a bigger assortment, they went to David's for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there something like the Chamber of Commerce now is among the businessmen in Moscow in the old days? The Chamber of Commerce?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Was there business, I'm wondering how the businesses got along, competed with each other and cooperated with each other, both in the early days, not just clothing but in general on Main Street.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think it was pretty fair. I don't think there was much old, there was a time when the drug stars, when Frank Robinson came in here and opened up his psyche on it. And he opened up a drug store down on the corner of 6th and Main, and he opened up another one on Hermikel Bussard. And, of course he disposed of both of them later, but the other drug stars were pretty put out about his system of doing business. But it's no different than today, the drug pharmacists have a lot of competition today. You've got your drug fur that came in here now that are appealing to pharmaceutical needs. But I think things in general up and down Main Street was pretty good, because they, their Chamber of Commerce, I don't know when the Chamber of Commerce was organized, but it was pretty early. And they cooperated very well in donations and sponsoring the Fourth of July, the fair, and different things that came to Moscow. Circuses, even had a Chautauqua here three different years that the city sponsored.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now up and down Main Street, you know, when we came here in Op. 2 there were a lot of false front buildings. By that I mean there was a false upper part to cover up the back part, so it made the store look like it was bigger than it was. But there are still standing permanent buildings that were there, are here now when we came here, and that's the Moscow Hotel, the telephone building, which is the old Scatterbove block, the Owl Drugstore, which was the SHIELD hardware store. And up above the hardware store was a business school run by T .N. Krikmar. He ran that for a good many years. Another fellow named Wilson carried it on. David's Store Building was built by Durham and Kaufman back in the late 80s. Must have been 1987 or eight around in there. The McConnell -McGuire Building was built back in the 80s. Creighton Store, that's an old building.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But there were a lot of wooden buildings on Main Street, an awful lot of wooden buildings. And of course it was the street, streets were paved on Main Street until about 1910. Before that time it was mud and then they covered the mud with rock. It was a pretty messy setup. And we didn't have cement sidewalks, we had board sidewalks, just like you found in the Pioneer towns.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The town, you know, was really divided into four sections in the early days. The center of town actually was a Main 6th. From 6th Street and Main, North and West was the New Ellen addition. And West and South was the Deacon division. And then up in Sweet Town, all that was the McGregor addition. And the other was the Russell addition. How they happened to, the 3rd and Main being the center, I don't know, but that happened to be where the property lines were. And they, as I understand it, donated the Main Street off of their property. And Russell of course donated the acre ground where the Russell School is. And McGregor owned the boarding house where the Gritman Hospital is, which he run a boarding house and hotel there for some years. And then it was converted into a hospital by Mr. Gritman. And he operated that until the present structure was built.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I think of having a blacksmith shop right where the Kenworthy Theater is. A blacksmith shop and a carriage shop. They were operated by M .F. Zumwalt, who was a character in the early days here in Moscow, and Major Collins, who did most of the horseshoeing and so on. And then Chris Anderson used to make wheels, repair carriages, and all that. That was right on Main Street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about Zumwalt? Why he was such a character?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, he was a boisterous type of a fellow. He was short tempered, but tender hearted at that. He was, I think, one of the first firemen on our fire department, which was a rural area. Volunteer. Volunteer fire department. We had three or four of them. They were in different sections of the town. We didn't have fire engines. They just had a wheel with holes until later on. But Zumwalt was, as I say, he was as honest as the day's wrong. But he was short tempered. He told you exactly what he thought, whether you liked it or not. Had a wonderful family of four girls. All went into the teaching field. I had one of them as a Latin teacher in high school.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And one of my first jobs that I had after my father passed away was piling six cords of sawed wood for M .F. Zumwalt into his woodshed, which he lived right back at the blacksmith shop. That was one of the first jobs I had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of tradition did they have about the old stores that had been there before you were there?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was thinking of Durham and Kaufman, what they thought of Durham and Kaufman when people talked about them in the pioneer times.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, Durham and Kaufman came out of the Midwest. And they were Jewish, both of them. And they knew the business angle from A to Z. And they had a store in, I forget what was it, Kendrick or Juliaetta also.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And they were, of course, food buyers.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And they knew how to make a profit on merchandise. Well, they were here during the wet year when they didn't get to harvest their wheat. And of course, they did a credit business with the farmers, just like Davis did later on. And some of those bills would run up twelve, fifteen hundred dollars and they couldn't pay it. So he would take a mortgage on their place, or a chattel mortgage. And from what I understand, they had a good many thousands of dollars tied up in those mortgages. Well, it got to the point, I guess, where they had to have money too, so they foreclosed on them. And it left a bitter taste in the whole community here, over the two. And it eventually practically drove them out of Moscow because of that. So they left here in the 90s sometime and went down to San Francisco and opened up the Emporium, which finally became the biggest retail store on the Pacific Coast. I've really been in the store, even when they had moved in there, of course. And today, of course, it's dwindled down now to what it used to be. And the original Durham Coughlin, even their families are out of it now too. But that, I would judge they were here about ten years, but I love that they accumulated quite a bit of money.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I've heard there was bad feeling towards Vollmer in the Troy area because of foreclosure during that period.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And they did too, especially around the Genesee area. In fact, the town of Troy used to be called Vollmer, you know. I think of that experience as why they changed it to Troy. But Vollmer did foreclose on, and that's where he got a lot of his land. But there were a lot of things that Vollmer did for this whole area when we were still in Nez Perce County. He was in the banking business as well as the real estate business. And if I'm not mistaken, he was a promoter of a railroad from Missoula down the Lewis and Clark Highway into Lewiston, which didn't pass. But he tried to promote that and eventually came through this way and down to Lewiston and then from Lewiston out to La Mota or whatever town is in the other direction.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about McConnell's store? Did they talk about that when you were a young man? About what kind of operation McConnell had?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, it was a complete department store, grocers and all. And they used all three floors of the building too. And it probably would have been in business today had Governor McConnell not accepted the governorship of Idaho. He went down and spent four years as governor down there. For some reason, it may have been the times, but of course that was during that wet year too. That's what affected him too. He was governor from 1893 to 96, four years. And by the way, Mr. F .A. David was head of the grocery department in the McConnell -McGuire store. He had money in it and he lost it. Because McConnell, the McGwire store did not go bankrupt like some people thought. He just simply closed it up and sold his merchandise and other mortgage, his own home and all that. And he paid his bills. Maybe he didn't pay 100 % on the dollar, but he didn't go into bankruptcy.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And after that experience, McConnell of course was elected to the Indian agency or something. And then later on he was an immigration officer for years here. Had his offices in the old post office building there.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Now they had a wonderful store up there and did a good business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You were telling me about the getting back and forth that went on between the groceries and the clothing in Davis.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, the grocery department was at the rear of the store and they carried high -grade chocolates, which all the help liked. And every once in a while when they weren't looking, we'd go back there and help ourselves to a chocolate. And then they even had a reserved stock of candy in our room that the elevator passed. And all you had to do was step off the elevator and go in there and help yourself learn. Well, it got to be so bad that the firm put a padlock on that door and instructed the groceryman to tell everybody to keep out of it. But that didn't entirely solve the problem. So one of the grocerymen decided to put some peregrine in some of the chocolates on the top. And they knew which ones they were. So one of the girls in the driver's department went back there and she got one of ours. And it sure made her sick. She had to go home. And her parents even came down to the store and took it up to the store that somebody had pulled something up. But they came out. Then they used to buy watermelons by the hay rack load and line them up on the floor. Well, there was a common opening between the driver's department and the manager's department and the grocery department. And of course they could sell a cracked watermelon. So every once in a while one of the fellows during watermelon season would go out down there and kick one of those watermelons to crack it. That way they had a watermelon feed.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And Mr. David, coming originally from Wisconsin, was a great cheese man. And he used to buy cheese for the carload.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And he would cure it. He'd get it fresh and he'd cure it on the top floor of the store.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And then bring it down. They were great big girls. They would weigh 500 pounds, I guess, on some of those cheese. And they'd put it on a cheese cutter. You'd come in and you'd buy any amount of cheese you want. But it was two bits for a dollar.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I remember sugar in those days. You'd get 100 pounds for $4 .95. Today you're paying $40 for 100 pounds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He used to cure cheese on the roof, did you say last time? Did you say he used to cure cheese on the roof?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, this was on the third floor. No, it wasn't on the roof. It was hot up there in the summertime. All of us in the store would go up there and turn them every so often so that they wouldn't get cured one -sided. That was quite a job. And then in late August,

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: they'd have the fraternity and sorority houses and the dormitories come down. And the canned goods people would have a display of their canned goods up on the third floor. And have samples and they'd go through and sample it. Some of it was water, some of it was sugar, and so on. And they'd buy their supply of canned goods for the winter that way. That's one reason the grocery store at David's was a very big part of their business in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did students have any trouble getting credit from David's?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: No, all they had to do was put a reference down, and that mostly was their folks. And in later years, they would write their folks and tell them that their son or daughter has come out and applied for credit. And we've accepted them, and we want your sanction as to whether they should have credit or for how much. And they got some of the finest letters back from parents thanking them for informing them about it. And in some cases, they let them have free reign, and in some cases, they put a limit on them, and so on. So it worked out very, very fine.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I'd like to change the subject and talk some about recreation and social life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And before talking about it in later years, one event of your childhood that I noticed here was, that you mentioned before, was the flood they had between Moscow and Poland, and you were a kid and saw that, right?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: That's right.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, that was around 1918.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And I've forgotten what time of the year, it seems like it was in January or February. We had a great thaw, and then right after the thaw, it froze stiffer than board. Everybody got up there, skates, and we were able to skate down the Pullman Highway. It isn't where it is now. It wasn't where it is now. But it was all flooded clear across there, and we could skate down the Pullman, and that lasted for about two weeks. Everybody was out on skates. I know I got a pair of curled toe English or German skis, or skates that I had used.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: So that was, I think, about the worst flood I think we've ever had. Oh, we may have had one more, that Pullman got pretty hard, where there was about three feet of water in the main street of Pullman, and sandbags didn't even hold it. But later years, they had trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was your social life like in the 20s in the community in those early years of World War I?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we had a Moscow band, for one thing. And I happened to be in the band and played cornet. And we had a hay rack, fixed up with seats. And every Friday night, we would play on Main Street, a different corner, every Friday night. So we'd each be in this house and have some publicity out of it. We did that for a good many years, and then some Sundays we used to get a concert in the park. Well, in 1912, our band was a pretty good band. Ed Carey was the leader. And the Rose Festival over at Portland. The Elks wanted to send a band over there, and it developed into a North Idaho band. So we had 48 musicians. And we went over to the Portland Road Show, and we competed against 48 bands and came home with second prize. On that, well, that was an incentive to keep the band going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music did you play?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, it was ragtime music. Oh, you beautiful doll, Alexander's Ragtime Band. And there was a new piece that came out at that time. And when the parade would stop, we'd play on corners and people would get out in the street and dance. We had a, it was a band of music that people liked. Had lots of harmony in it. And they enjoyed it. I know evenings we used to go around with the different wrath killers and play. And people would be dancing on top of the tables even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that interesting? How many were you in the band?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I was in the band.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many were there in that?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, I think we had about, I think it was 48 pieces in the band. That is our city band wasn't that big, no. No, that's what I meant. No, our city band was 20 some odd pieces. But we enjoyed it. And then from that, of course, we formed a dance orchestra, which I played in at the old Egan's Hall. Ten cents a dance. And there were evenings that we would make as much as ten, twelve, fifteen dollars a night a piece. Plan for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you keep the numbers pretty short?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You didn't get any encores, I'll tell you. And then our Moscow band did lots of playing around other territories here. We went up to Potlach and played for their celebrations. They used to have a lot of celebrations there during the real lumber guns up there. We went over to Colfax, down to Troy, and around.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I've been told that some religious groups had a pretty strong gripe about dancing at the time. And didn't feel that it was good for people in Moscow.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, there was a fellow from Spokane came down here and started a mandolin and guitar club. And seems to me, he gave them all lessons in one class. He didn't take you into bed, really. And I'd forgotten, there must have been twenty -five or thirty of us that took those lessons. And then after we had completed the course, I think he gave us eight or ten lessons. He gave a concert in the Methodist Church. Well, that disbanded the organization, but they were my sister and myself. And Signe Egan and Jenny Peterson formed a little group of our own. And we played around at lodges and churches and young people's places of pieces that we played. Well, we were invited to play at the Lutheran Church one Sunday, which we did. And of course we played waltzes and two -steps. Well, some of the more conservative Lutherans thought it was out of place. And we got a little criticism on that, so we never played at a church after that.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I guess what I heard recently was that there was some feeling that if they didn't like drink, they felt that any drinking was wrong. They might also feel that dancing was wrong, perhaps. But some people felt that way, that they were both wrong.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, the Methodist Church were pretty strict on dancing and playing cards in the early days here. That was taboo. And I think it wasn't until up in the 20s, I guess, before dancing generally got to be more popular. I know when the Mormons came in here in the 20s, they're great on youth work. And they allowed dancing in their recreational room and things of that type. And that helped things out a little. So that there was a group that was against both dancing and cards. But we've got a different civilization today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your work with the Scouts? When did that start in?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, that started around the Sunday school class in the Presbyterian church in 1920. There was a lady teaching that had seven boys. And they were little boys that had a lot on the ball. And she was at loss to know what to entertain them with. So she asked me if I would help her out, which I did. And I formed a little athletic club around her class, which we met every week. And then we took a few hikes and so on. A little later on, there was a scout troop in town here. And he became an executive. And he was interested in organizing the town and scouting. So he came to me and asked me if I would like to convert that class into a scout troop. And then he wanted to. So I wouldn't go along with him at first because we were having a good program as it was. But I eventually fell for it. And I knew nothing about scouting. So when we went into it, we started out with these seven boys. And we built it up to 32 boys of the community. They weren't all Presbyterians by any means.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And we had a very interesting program. We had good backing from the church and from the parents. And through that, we developed a campsite out in Moscow Mountain, which we used quite often. And every year, between Christmas and New Year's, we'd go out for a three -day stay in the mountains in the wintertime. Take our skis and toboggans out there. Well, the one year, I was just overloaded with boys. I took boys in from other troops. We had 48 boys out there. We went out there in dry weather. Went out in cars. Some of the boys hiked up. When we were up there, it snowed. And my parents got a little concerned about us being up there. So they got a hold of a couple of bulb sleds and went out there to bring us back to town. Well, the camp was over, and we were just getting ready to hike back in. And they picked us up. But the nice thing about that particular campsite was that we had a cabin up there, a house up there, that a banker had built years ago as kind of a summer resort. And he abandoned them, and we fixed it up. Put bunks in there and got bed springs from town. And we housed all the boys in that house except 12. And they had to build lindoos outside, which they'd been taught how to build. And they slept out in the open, which they got a lark on at 11. And feeding them was quite a problem, because some boys liked some things and some didn't. And I had my older scouts act as cooks with supervision. And we had good meals, and they enjoyed them. And we wound up to the last day when we wanted to get rid of everything. So we had a great big cattle. We had stew, made stew in. So we took bread, we took eggs that we had left, and we had peas and potatoes.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Anyway, we dumped the whole works into the pot and let it boil. And there wasn't a boy in the camp, and didn't think it was great. Some of the boys that were picayune -es wouldn't eat this or that, had to eat it or starve. And they found out that they liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kinds of skills did you teach the boys in scouting then?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, it was the regular scout program of trustworthiness, loyal, helpful, friendly, kind, obedient, cheerful, brave, clean, and reverend. Those were all taught them with the different tests that they took, like cooking, fire building, hiking, swimming, schoolwork, religious work, all combined into the program that made a character -building organization out of it. And out of that, I had one, two, I had three scout troops in my time, and those troop commandment the rest of the time, because I spent about 30 years in scouting. And when I completed it all, I got the highest award that scouting gives you, the Silver Beaver, which was a word of me later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was a lot more than camping skills, but were camping skills very much a part of it, to get to get along, survive in the outdoors?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, hiking and camping, because nature is one of the programs that's in scouting, and there is one test that every boy has to take a 14 -mile hike and make observations on his hike. What he saw, perform a good turn on that hike, and return in the same day. And then write up a 200 -word theme on what he saw and did. I have dozens and dozens of those filed away now that when some of these older scouts come here and visit with me, I get those out and I read them to them to see whether they remember what they did on that 14 -mile hike. Now I think it's a wonderful program, and of course all boys don't enter into it fullheartedly, but most of them do. And for that reason, in 1931 or 1932, in Kiwanis Club, I was a member of Kiwanis. Oh, that was early on, that was in 1926.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Scouting had dropped tremendously in the community here, through financing and one thing or other. It was dead for a year, except the troops that I had, which met in my basement.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And Kiwanis had just started in 1926, and they needed a boys and girls program, which is one of the national programs in Kiwanis. So I was at the meeting and I proposed to them if they wanted to put scouting back on the map here in Moscow, I'd be willing to see what I could do about it and head a duck. Well, they just fell for it like a duck suit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they said, well, how much do you think it will cost?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I said, well, I think it will start off with about $200. So they went along with me and we used that $200 to set up five scout troops. We had 125 boys in scouting in, I would say, three months.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It went over so big that the community then went into the scout council, which was in Lewiston. And we've had scouting in Moscow ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you at all involved in the starting up of Grizzly Camp as a boy scout camp?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yes, it was camp layered first, and then was later called. I spent several weeks up there when they had their summer camp. It was a very satisfactory place, and it is today a very good scout camp for boys.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: One problem we had in scouting, most boys don't go into scouting, or don't take up scouting after they're 15 years old. That meant they had three years of scouting, and a lot of them didn't want to quit. So there was a young fellow at the university who left to be a sea scout. We knew nothing about sea scouting. He came to me and suggested that we start a sea scout of these older boys, because that takes in boys from 15 on up. So we immediately organized a scout troop, or a sea scout troop, out of these older boys. And they had, oh I imagine, 20, 25 boys in that program, which helped to carry on their scouting program. Well, he operated for about three years, and of course he was here at the university, and then it was taken up by somebody else, and then it finally petered out for lack of leadership. But the nice thing about the sea scout group, they hadn't mentioned the name of it, the Sea Scout Samson, which I was proud of. Well, I started to say on Kiwanis, there was one thing Kiwanis did too.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: There were a number of people in town here who felt that we were not getting the boys that ought to be in scouting. Some of the, not particularly underprivileged, but boys that thought scouting was sissy and so on. So I proposed that we see what we could do and find out who those boys were. So I went to the superintendent of schools, Fulton Gale, and asked him for the name of boys that were giving him trouble. These were boys that were in the seventh, ninth grade, and probably freshman in high school. He gave me a list of about nine, ten boys that were giving him trouble. So I went back to the Boys and Girls Work Committee at Kiwanis and told them this. So I says, if you want to head it up, we'll go ahead and back it. So that was another two hundred dollars. So we formed an athletic club and we had our bylaws based pretty much on the scout program. Only worded different. And I operated that the first year, and Kelly Kline, or Hod Halpert the next year, and Kelly Kline the third year. And we had a very fine baseball team and a basketball team. We played different teams around in the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This group came from the troublemaking kids?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I went to these boys that were the troublemakers and I says, I told them what kind of a program we would put up for them. And they went for it and I says, now we need more boys than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now can you get more boys to join the group?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well of course they went out and got their same kind. So we had a baseball team with a reserve on the bench of about six. And a basketball team with reserves. And it worked out very fine. I thought it was a minor good program and it was something that was suggested to me that we weren't reaching those kind of boys.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, there's one question that I ask a lot of people about the old times and today. And that is how you think it compares or what we're looking back on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the best times were for Moscow? The best times?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Yeah. Well from a business standpoint, we had what you'd call a recession now in 1921 and 2. Along in there. And it affected the whole community because businesses dropped considerably. Well, we got over that and immediately business went right up to 1929. We had some of our best business years then. And then of course when the depression come on in 1931 and 2, well, we all had to take salary cuts. Business dropped almost 50 % in sales. And it took us actually up until World War II before business got back on its feet again. And then of course things just popped during World War II. Then after World War II was over, business was, I think, the best business that Moscow has ever had along 48 and 9. In fact, the store had its biggest increase in business during those years. Then we had another slight recession when Eisenhower went in. And that didn't last, that didn't amount to anything. And then when Kennedy went in, there was a slight recession. And then when Johnson went in, things started picking up again. We had good business during the latter 60s.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: But I think as a whole, Moscow and late Tarkov has been very fortunate through all the business cycles except the one in 1993. They've come out of all of them. We've had bankruptcies, there have been few.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And that's a pretty good place for business. And still is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, looking at social problems that we have today, like we were talking before about the energy problem, what do you think about that as far as what it means for the future of this area?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, we know that the world needs agriculture. They need wheat, barley, oats, peas, and all that. We know that the farmer is not going to be let down on energy to produce a crop. We know he's going to be all right. The rest of us may have to tighten up and do a little more walking, probably ride a bicycle. And cut our heat down a little. Not waste as much as we're doing. Probably raise more garden stuff. I think all those things we're going to have to do in the next few years if we're going to come out alive on those things. Because really I think we're headed for a real depression. This recession they call now is really the beginning of it. And usually those things start in the fall of the year. Stock market goes down in the fall.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And economists don't agree. Some of them say we're going to have it, some say we're not. Some say it's going to level off. And others say we need a depression to get people back to their senses.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Just like you were saying on people driving fifty -five miles an hour, they forgot about that. A lot of them now they're driving seventy and seventy -five. Just because we've got plenty of it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the troubles, the economic troubles are really of recent origin or do you think the problem is long standing?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, they've been building up for years. They've been building up ever since 1932 when Roosevelt went in. He started things in the first place. And while he saved a lot of businesses when he put a moratorium on the banks and mortgages, one thing or another, he didn't solve the problem entirely. Maybe it's something that's going to happen every generation that way.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: And as far as right now is concerned, I think our federal government is responsible for deflation, or if we have a depression, are responsible for it probably more so than the people themselves because they've encouraged them to spend more money, except federal money. Look at the programs I've got today that if you build a courthouse, we'll furnish seventy percent of it. Or if you build a new school building, there's programs for that. And the government's just overspending themselves and they're forcing it upon the people. But people could get along without that if they wanted to. But now that it's a common thing to run into the federal government, back you on just those things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think about the role of businesses in something like, as we were talking about, the problems with the economy?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: You know what I'm saying. Well, what I mean is, you were saying to me before that it's natural for everybody to want profit, do as well as they can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how do you deal with the problem of trying to conserve energy and that sort of thing?

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, I say when you put controls on, people try to work around them. It's just like prohibition days. When they cut out liquor, you had the bootleggers. And it's no different today. People themselves are going to have a different attitude toward the whole problem rather than live today and let tomorrow take over itself.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Well, there's one place that I shouldn't fail to mention and that's the gallop, which is called the Pleasant Home, run by Mr. Gallop on the corner of Jackson and Third. It's a boarding house where you get a meal for 25 cents and he had the roomers upstairs. His son, Harry Gallop, was the one that had the gallop lumber yard at one time. It was an institution there for years and years he operated and did a good business. There's Judge Hodgins that operated Hodgins Drug Store over all the years. He originally came to Moscow from Genesee, was a teacher, a farmer, and learned pharmacy through a druggist over there. He came over here and bought the white drug store, which was the first drug store here, and expanded it and got into books and stationery and whatnot. And it was the days when you rolled a lot of your own pills. You had your own scale and measuring device. Well, he was a prominent Democrat in politics and he was quite a community man. He and Mr. David always bet a suit of clothes on the national convention, or national present election. And I think Mr. David lost more suits because at that time we hadn't a good many Democrats in. Well, then there was the Moscow Hotel, which was run by Mr. Gale. His daughter married Gov. Nix here in Moscow. He operated that for a good many years in a manner like fashion. Had a restaurant connected with it, the pool hall, the barber shop, and three floors. Then across the street kind of corner was the France and Shields hardware store. That was operated before 1900. And MJ Shields, who was next door, who was quite a party, ran an implement house and also operated the first electric light plant in Moscow, which was located down where the standard lumber company is today. He was a character in that while he lived only about eight blocks from his business, he drove his horse and buggy to work. And he had an entrance in the front of his store where he could drive his rig right into the store, and that's where J .C. Penney is today.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I mentioned about Zunhof and Major Collins. Then there was C .B. Green, who had the first, not the first laundry, because the first laundry here was a Chinese laundry. He operated for the laundry is today for years. He did a remarkable business and he was a wonderful community man. Then there was Emmy Lewis, who was in the insurance and real estate business. He was responsible for a lot of the early development of Moscow.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: O .C. Carcival, I certainly have to mention that. He was one of the early grocers here down where Powell Plumbing was yesterday.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: The whole family operated the grocery store. He and his wife and his youngsters. It was a place that was just a jumble of groceries around. You just stumbled through things to find what you wanted, but he had everything and he did a wonderful business. His wife, after he passed away in later years, became a reporter for the Star -Mirror and later for the Idahoians. She has a collection of Star -Mirror and Idaho Star -Mirror papers that she gave to the museum, which are up there now, but we haven't gone through yet. I remember there was George Weber, who was probably one of the oldest pioneers around here. He came from Genesee over to Moscow and opened up a harness shop and fitted hundreds and hundreds of horses with equipment and saddles and so on. I mentioned Hawken Belegard. He came here in 1901 in the bank, which was the first trust company then, and he operated it until it was sold to the first security bank here.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Then there's McGregor. I mentioned that he built the boarding house where the Gritman Hospital is. Max Griffith came here in the early 1900s, 1902, 1904, and run the credit association and a law practice together. There's Charlie Thompson, who was in the title business. The title building where your offices have been is where they operated for years and years. He and his brother.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Herman Wilson came to Moscow from Walla Walla. He had experience in merchandising, and he did a little farming around Moscow. Then Montarquiller, which was a chain store, opened up in the tuna building after N. Williamson closed down. It was empty for a good many years. He operated under the Montarquiller chain for a while until later years. He got into the pea business with his father -in -law Washburn. Washburn had a grocery store, and with his financing, they opened up this pea processing plant where it is today. He built up the international business on peas, even to Russia, South America, all over the world. It was a tremendous big business. Just how it, well, he passed away and the business was sold or turned over to someone else.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: It's never been what it was when it first turned out. But Herman Wilson was a very community -minded man and did a lot for Moscow.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: One of the early doctors in town, Dr. Crithers and Dr. Gritman, of course, were the two earliest. Later on, there was Dr. Ashbury, Dr. Clark, Dr. Barrows, Mr. Litch, Dr. Adair, Dr. Hatfield. And one of our earliest dentists was McCauley. That was before my time, but in my time, McBride was the earliest. And McDaniel, and Fainter, and Dr. Boyd.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, Nealey's, uh, uh, the Liberty Barn. That's McNealey's father. They came to Moscow in the 80s and located out here at Cornwall. And then he came into town and he had a livery stable down there on Jackson for years. And then when the automobile business came in, they operated a taxi and an automobile agency. And they operated that until, well, they cut out the taxi business, but they still had an automobile business until the city bought it for a parking lot. So they were in the delivery business for a good many, many years. Because everything was horse and buggy in those days. It would make a difference if you wanted to go to Genesee, Colton, Uniontown, or wherever, you had to go buy horse and buggy.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, we'll see.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Oh, Adrian Nelson, Judge Adrian Nelson, uh, was, uh, one of the early graduates of the University of Idaho. And, uh, he opened up a law practice and was probate judge. Oh, for many years, until he, uh, been retired, but he quit and opened up a law practice downtown. He had a big, uh, big family, all of them graduating from the university. All have been prominent in their field, education, business, even, uh, the ministry. So that, uh, he was, uh, an early, real early pioneer.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: Collins and Orleans store, Mr. Collins, uh, was, uh, one of the, uh, first deputy sheriffs, and his deputy sheriff, of Lake Delano County in the early days. And, uh, he and Collins, and he and Orleans run a hardware store right next to Hodgins Drug Store for years until Collins sold out to Orleans and then moved over on Third Street.

speaker: Harry Sampson
words: I guess that's all.
